title: Floating point, rounding & numerical errors in MATLAB
summary: Teaches MATLAB finite arithmetic exactly as in Session 1: floating-point representation $a=(-1)^s pN^q$, machine epsilon and machine precision, absolute vs relative error, the rounding-error bound, condition number, and catastrophic cancellation. Builds the Session 1 finite-difference derivative code (`(sin(x+h)-sin(x))./h` with element-wise operators, log-log error plot) and its cancellation-free prosthaphaeresis form, walks through the condition-number QUIZ (`tmax=-log10(2*E_min)+1` giving t=15), and covers machine subtraction and overflow-avoidance, all with runnable snippets quoted from `session1_solutions_2025.m`.
objectives: Write the floating-point representation $a=(-1)^s pN^q$, identify normalized mantissa and exponent, and state what machine numbers are
Define and distinguish machine epsilon `eps`$=N^{1-t}$ from machine precision $\varepsilon_m=\tfrac12 N^{1-t}$, and recall MATLAB's `eps`, `realmin`, `realmax`
Compute absolute and relative errors in MATLAB with `abs(...)`/`abs(...)` and explain why relative error is preferred
Reproduce the Session 1 finite-difference derivative code, including element-wise `.*`, `./`, `.^`, and the prosthaphaeresis reformulation that removes cancellation
Interpret log-log / semilogy error curves and explain why the naive difference-quotient error grows as the step $h$ shrinks
Solve the QUIZ relating the condition number $K$ to the maximum mantissa digits $t$ via `tmax=-log10(2*E_min)+1`
Recognize and avoid overflow and catastrophic cancellation by reformulating expressions (e.g. `prod(x+1:z)` for factorials)

blocks.title: Why floating point matters in this course

blocks.text: The MATLAB part of the exam opens with **finite arithmetic**: how a computer stores real numbers, the errors that creates, and how those errors can blow up. The course's own words: *"One of the worst errors to avoid when programming with a computer is thinking that the so-obtained numerical results are correct or affected by negligible errors."* You will be asked to reproduce, exactly, the Session 1 commands that expose these errors. This topic teaches every command from `session1_solutions_2025.m` and the theory behind it.

A real number $a$ is stored as $$a = (-1)^s\, p\, N^{q},$$ where $s\in\{0,1\}$ is the **sign**, $N$ is the **basis** ($N=2$ binary, $N=10$ decimal), $p\ge 0$ is the **mantissa**, and $q$ is the integer **exponent** (characteristic). The representation is **normalized** when $N^{-1}\le p < 1$, which makes $p$ and $q$ unique. For $N=10$ this means $0.1 \le p < 1$.

blocks.title: Reading off mantissa and exponent ($N=10$)
blocks.text: From the slides: $a = 0.15\cdot 10^{-2}$ has mantissa $p=0.15$, exponent $q=-2$. And $a = 12.4 = 0.124\cdot 10^{2}$ has $p=0.124$, $q=2$. Normalization is just "shift the point so the first digit after it is non-zero."

Only finitely many reals fit. The set of **machine numbers** is $$F=\{0\}\cup\{(-1)^s\,0.a_1a_2\dots a_t\cdot N^q,\; a_1\neq 0,\; L\le q\le U\}.$$ Here $t$ is the maximum number of **mantissa digits**, and $L\le q\le U$ bounds the exponent. A real with more than $t$ mantissa digits must be **rounded** to $t$ digits.

blocks.title: What MATLAB actually uses (IEEE 754 double)
blocks.text: MATLAB uses IEEE 754 double precision: basis $N=2$, 64 bits = 1 sign bit, 52 stored mantissa bits (53 effective), 11 exponent bits. The 53 binary mantissa bits are about **16 decimal digits**. Useful built-ins: `realmin` $\approx 2.2\cdot10^{-308}$ (smallest positive machine number), `realmax` $\approx 1.8\cdot10^{308}$ (largest), `eps` $\approx 2.2\cdot10^{-16}$ (machine epsilon).

The **rounding error** when storing $a$ as its machine number $\bar a$ is bounded by $$\frac{|a-\bar a|}{|a|}=\frac{|p-\bar p|}{|p|}\le \tfrac12 N^{1-t}.$$ The course defines $$\texttt{eps}=N^{1-t}\quad\text{(machine epsilon)},\qquad \varepsilon_m=\tfrac12 N^{1-t}\quad\text{(machine precision)}.$$ So $\varepsilon_m$ is the **maximum relative error** of storing one number, and $\bar a = a(1+\varepsilon)$ with $|\varepsilon|\le\varepsilon_m$.

blocks.title: eps vs machine precision — don't confuse them
blocks.text: In this course `eps` $=N^{1-t}$ but the **machine precision** is $\varepsilon_m=\tfrac12 N^{1-t}$ — half of `eps`. The relative rounding-error bound is $\tfrac12 N^{1-t}=\varepsilon_m$, NOT `eps`. The factor $\tfrac12$ is exactly what the QUIZ exercise hinges on.

If $\tilde x$ approximates $x$: the **absolute error** is $e_a=|x-\tilde x|$ and the **relative error** is $e_r=\dfrac{|x-\tilde x|}{|x|}$ (for $x\neq 0$). Relative error is the meaningful one: with $x=10^3,\tilde x=999$ we get $e_a=1$ but $e_r=10^{-3}$; with $x=10^6,\tilde x=999999$ again $e_a=1$ but $e_r=10^{-6}$, far smaller. In MATLAB the relative error of an approximation `r` to the true `cos(x)` is `abs(cos(x)-r)/abs(cos(x))`.

Exercise 1.1 of Session 1 approximates $f'(x)$ for $f(x)=\sin x$ by the difference quotient $$r(x)=\frac{\sin(x+h)-\sin(x)}{h},$$ with $h=2^{-k}$, $k=1,\dots,50$, at $x=\pi/4$. Mathematically smaller $h$ means a better derivative. Numerically, the subtraction $\sin(x+h)-\sin(x)$ for tiny $h$ subtracts two **almost equal** numbers — **catastrophic cancellation** — and the error eventually grows as $h$ shrinks.

steps.label: Set the point and step sizes
steps.text: `x = pi/4;` then `k = 1:50;` and `h = 2.^(-k);` builds the row vector $h=2^{-k}$. Note `2.^(-k)` uses element-wise power `.^` so every entry of `k` is exponentiated.

steps.label: Naive difference quotient
steps.text: `r = (sin(x+h)-sin(x))./h;` — element-wise `./` divides each entry. This is the cancellation-prone form.

steps.label: Relative error vs the exact derivative
Plot on log-log axes
steps.text: `loglog(h,err,'r','linewidth',2)` plots error vs step on logarithmic x and y axes — the only way to see error spanning many orders of magnitude. `'r'` = red curve, `'linewidth',2` = thicker line. `pause` waits for a keypress; `hold on` keeps the figure so the next curve overlays.

steps.label: Cancellation-free reformulation (prosthaphaeresis)
steps.text: Using $\sin(x+h)-\sin(x)=2\sin(h/2)\cos(x+h/2)$: `r2 = 2*sin(h/2).*cos(x+h/2)./h;`. Here `.*` multiplies element-wise. Then `err2 = abs(cos(x)-r2)/abs(cos(x));` and `loglog(h,err2,'g','linewidth',2)` overlays the green curve.

blocks.title: What the two curves show
blocks.text: The naive red curve first **decreases** (truncation error $\sim h$ dominates) then **increases** as $h\to 0$ because cancellation in $\sin(x+h)-\sin(x)$ amplifies rounding error $\sim \varepsilon_m/h$. The green prosthaphaeresis curve avoids the subtraction of near-equal numbers, so it keeps decreasing and stays near machine precision. Same math, different numerical stability.

blocks.title: `.*` `./` `.^` are element-wise — not optional
blocks.text: `h = 2.^(-k)`, `r = (...)./h`, `r2 = 2*sin(h/2).*cos(x+h/2)./h` all operate on whole vectors at once. Writing `*`, `/`, `^` instead would attempt matrix operations and either error or give the wrong answer. Element-wise dot-operators are the single most common MATLAB exam mistake.

question.prompt: In `r = (sin(x+h)-sin(x))./h;` with `h = 2.^(-k)` and `k = 1:50`, why is `./` used instead of `/`?

options.content: `/` would round the result to an integer
`h` is a 1x50 vector, so element-wise division is needed to divide each difference by its own step
`./` is faster but otherwise identical to `/`

question.explanation: `h` is a row vector of 50 step sizes, so `sin(x+h)-sin(x)` is also a 50-element vector; `./` divides each entry by the corresponding `h`. `/` would attempt matrix right-division (solving a linear system) and not give the per-step quotient. A is wrong (no integer rounding happens); C is wrong (`/` is genuinely a different operation, not just slower); D is wrong (`/` is valid but means matrix division).
question.theory: Element-wise operators `.*`, `./`, `.^` act entrywise on arrays; bare `*`, `/`, `^` are matrix operations.

blocks.title: The QUIZ — condition number and digits of mantissa

blocks.text: Session 1 QUIZ: floating-point arithmetic with basis $N=10$ and $t$ mantissa digits. The problem $y=f(x)$ has **condition number** $K=2\times 10^9$. A datum $\bar x$ is affected by rounding error and $\bar y=f(\bar x)$ gives $\dfrac{|y-\bar y|}{|y|}=10^{-5}$. How many mantissa digits $t$ (at maximum)?

The condition number $K$ bounds how the relative output error follows the relative input error: $$\frac{|y-\bar y|}{|y|}\le K\,\frac{|x-\bar x|}{|x|}.$$ $K$ small $\Rightarrow$ **well-conditioned** (input errors not amplified); $K$ large $\Rightarrow$ **ill-conditioned**. It is a property of the problem $f$, not of the algorithm. (Algorithm quality is **stability**.)

steps.label: Lower-bound the input error
steps.text: From $\frac{|y-\bar y|}{|y|}\le K\frac{|x-\bar x|}{|x|}$, isolate the input error: $\frac{|x-\bar x|}{|x|}\ge \frac{1}{K}\cdot\frac{|y-\bar y|}{|y|}$. In code: `K=2e9;` then `E_min=1e-5/K;` so $E_{\min}=\frac{10^{-5}}{2\times10^9}=0.5\times10^{-14}$.

steps.label: Connect to the rounding bound
steps.text: The input rounding error obeys $\frac{|x-\bar x|}{|x|}\le \tfrac12 N^{1-t}$. Chaining: $E_{\min}\le \tfrac12 N^{1-t}$, i.e. $2E_{\min}\le N^{1-t}$.

blocks.title: The exact QUIZ code
blocks.text:
```matlab
K=2e9;
E_min=1e-5/K;            % from |y-yb|/|y| <= K|x-xb|/|x|
% E_min <= 0.5*N^(1-t)  ->  2*E_min <= N^(1-t)
% log10(2*E_min) <= 1-t  ->  t <= -log10(2*E_min)+1
tmax=-log10(2*E_min)+1   % = 15  -> answer (b)
```
Note `log10` (base 10) is used because $N=10$, and the `+1` comes from the $N^{1-t}$ exponent. The $\tfrac12$ factor (machine precision, not `eps`) is what turns $E_{\min}=0.5\times10^{-14}$ into $2E_{\min}=10^{-14}$.

question.prompt: In the QUIZ, why does the solution use `t <= -log10(2*E_min)+1` rather than `-log10(E_min)+1`?

options.content: To convert from natural log to base-10 log
Because the relative rounding-error bound is the machine precision $\tfrac12 N^{1-t}$, so $E_{\min}\le \tfrac12 N^{1-t}$ gives $2E_{\min}\le N^{1-t}$
It is an arbitrary safety margin of factor 2

question.explanation: The rounding error obeys $\frac{|x-\bar x|}{|x|}\le \tfrac12 N^{1-t}=\varepsilon_m$. Multiplying both sides by 2 removes the $\tfrac12$, giving $2E_{\min}\le N^{1-t}$, hence the `2*E_min` inside the log. A is wrong (`log10` already is base 10, the 2 is unrelated); C is wrong ($N=10$ here, which is why `log10` is used); D is wrong (the 2 is the exact reciprocal of the $\tfrac12$ in the machine-precision bound, not arbitrary).
question.theory: Relative rounding error is bounded by machine precision $\varepsilon_m=\tfrac12 N^{1-t}$, half of machine epsilon $\texttt{eps}=N^{1-t}$.

blocks.title: Reading raw cancellation: machine subtraction and the (x!)^2 quiz
Machine subtraction with t=5 (Additional 1)
blocks.text:
With `format short e` (5-significant-digit exponential display): subtract $a=1.483593$, $b=1.484111$. The course first rounds each to 5 mantissa digits then subtracts:
```matlab
format short e
a = 1.483593;  b = 1.484111;
s  = a-b              % exact-ish difference
a_ = 1.4836;  b_ = 1.4841;   % rounded to 5 digits
s_ = a_-b_            % machine subtraction
er = abs(s-s_)/abs(s) % relative error of the cancelled result
```
The near-equal operands lose leading digits, so `er` is large — cancellation in action. `format short e` / `format long e` only change display, not the stored value.

blocks.title: Overflow and reformulation (Additional 4 quiz)
blocks.text:
The quantity $\frac{(x!)^2+(y!)^2}{z!}$ with $x=121,y=122,z=150$ **overflows** if computed directly ($121!$ is astronomically large $\Rightarrow$ `Inf`). Reformulate so each factorial is divided down before it explodes:
```matlab
x=121; y=122; z=150;
q = factorial(x)/prod(x+1:z) + factorial(y)/prod(y+1:z)
```
Here `prod(x+1:z)` multiplies all integers from $x+1$ to $z$, so $\frac{x!}{\text{prod}(122{:}150)}=\frac{x!\,z!^{-1}\cdot z!}{\dots}$ keeps numbers in range. This is the overflow-avoidance trick (same idea as computing $\sqrt{x_1^2+x_2^2}$ as $|x_1|\sqrt{1+(x_2/x_1)^2}$).

blocks.title: semilogy vs loglog
blocks.text: `loglog(h,err)` uses log scale on BOTH axes — use it when the x-data (`h`) also spans many magnitudes. `semilogy(1:m,err)` uses a log y-axis but linear x-axis — used in the $\pi$-approximation exercise where the x-axis is just the index `1:m`. Pick the one matching whether your x-values are spread over orders of magnitude.

Across all the cancellation exercises the recurring MATLAB pattern is identical: build `x = 10.^(-n)` (a vector of shrinking inputs), compute the naive formula `f`, compute a cancellation-free `f_ex` taken as exact, form `er = abs(f-f_ex)./abs(f_ex)`, display `[x' f' er']` and `loglog(x,er,'linewidth',2)`. Master this five-line skeleton and you can solve every Session 1 cancellation problem.
